head.title1: The Cuisine of Carnia — Il Bearč | Cjarsons, frico and traditional products
home.p2: A mountain cuisine born of necessity and ingenuity: few ingredients, the milk of the mountain dairies, the herbs of the meadows and the spices that emigrants brought back from afar.
global.p2: Carnian cuisine was born in the homes of the past, where women transformed a few humble ingredients into festive dishes. Milk and butter from the mountain dairies, polenta, beans, wild herbs gathered in the meadows, and the precious spices that arrived with the comings and goings between Carnia and Venice. From this meeting of poverty and ingenuity was born the signature dish of Carnia: the cjarsons.
cjarsons.p2: They go by two names, depending on the part of Carnia: cjarsons or cjalsons (the spellings cjalzons and cjalsòns also occur). They are a filled pasta parcel unlike any other: a filling in which sweet and savoury live side by side. Ricotta, aromatic herbs — preferably those with a lemony scent, such as scented geranium — raisins, chocolate, cinnamon, crushed biscuits, lemon zest. The proportions change from village to village, and even from family to family.
cjarsons.p3: Legend has it that the recipe was taught by the Guriut, a sweet-toothed sprite caught by a housewife as he stole the cream from freshly drawn milk. To earn her forgiveness, he revealed the secret of the cjarsons. Behind the fairy tale lies a true story. The Cramars, Carnia's itinerant merchants, from the late sixteenth century to the nineteenth stocked up on spices at the Venetian markets and worked their way from house to house up to Carnia and Austria: what stayed at home was whatever remained of that trade. Tradition has it that spices also arrived by a second route, with the young Carnian women who went into service in Venice. From the leftovers of these lives grew Carnia's passion for spices, which still perfumes the cjarsons filling today: cinnamon, nutmeg, cocoa.
cjarsons.p4: The finishing touch is always the same: scuete fumade e l'ont — grated smoked ricotta and melted butter.
cjarsons.p5: A local gastronomist has recorded over fifty variations. On the first Sunday of June every year, the village of Sutrio dedicates to the cjarsons a festival that brings together ten different versions, one for each village of Carnia.
cjarsons.p6: Sources: I Cjarsons — la tradizione della Carnia, Sutrio 2016 (Albergo Diffuso Borgo Soandri, ProLoco Sutrio, Municipality of Sutrio); Il Cibario del Friuli Venezia Giulia, ERSA 2017.
affumicatura.p1: Where the smoky taste comes from
affumicatura.p2: In the old days, in the houses of Carnia, hams, cured meats and ricotta were smoked slowly above the fogolâr, the traditional domestic hearth. The cheese wheels and the meats were hung above the fire, where the beech-wood smoke wrapped them little by little. That smoke, combined with long maturing, gave a delicate, unmistakable aroma, and at the same time helped preserve the products through the long mountain winters.
affumicatura.p3: This technique gave rise to the scuete fumade, the smoked ricotta that still perfumes the cjarsons today, and to the characteristic taste of the cured meats of the Carnian valleys, including the celebrated Prosciutto di Sauris PGI. Some artisan producers still draw inspiration from that ancient method, while relying on modern facilities that guarantee quality and safety.
prodotti.p2: Many of these products are listed in the Italian national register of Traditional Agri-food Products (PAT). Here are the ones that embody the gastronomic identity of these valleys.
prodotti.p3: The quintessential Carnian dish: melted cheese, on its own (crispy) or with potatoes and onion (soft). It is documented as early as the fifteenth century, in the recipe of Maestro Martino, cook to the Patriarch of Aquileia.
prodotti.p4: A cheese made by kneading different crumbled dairy wheels with salt, pepper and milk. Born so that nothing would go to waste: thrift turned into flavour. One of the most distinctive products of Carnia.
prodotti.p5: Ricotta smoked over the mountain-dairy fire: grated, it is the essential topping for cjarsons and many first courses. Together with melted butter (l'ont) it defines the taste of Carnian cuisine.
prodotti.p6: Corn cobs dried in braids in the open air. Polenta is the base that accompanies frico, toc' and soups: the daily bread of the mountains, before bread itself.
prodotti.p7: Among the most representative vegetables of Carnian agriculture, prized for being shelled by hand. The base of the minestrone and soups that warmed the mountain winters.
prodotti.p8: A typical Carnian product, grown in Verzegnis, a few kilometres from Socchieve. The basis of brovadìn, the Carnian version of brovada made without grape pomace, which was lacking here.
prodotti.p9: Production is concentrated above all in Sauris, where the gentle smoking uses beech wood only. Prosciutto di Sauris has held PGI status since 2010, the recognition that protects its origin and production method.
prodotti.p10: Produced with its own tradition throughout Carnia, with light beech-wood smoking and long maturing. Sauris stands out among the finest, where the Alpine climate and artisan craftsmanship give an elegant, delicate aroma, different from that of South Tyrolean speck.
prodotti.p11: A berry extremely rich in vitamin C that grows along the gravel beds of Carnian rivers. It is made into syrup and jam. It was once gathered by women and sold to Swiss pharmacies.
prodotti.p12: Entries drawn from Il Cibario del Friuli Venezia Giulia — Atlante dei Prodotti della Tradizione, edited by Cesare Corradini and Nadia Innocente, ERSA, 2017 (products of the PAT register, Ministerial Decree 12/12/16). The names in italics are in the Friulian language.
global.p4: Modern Carnian cuisine owes much to Gianni Cosetti (1939–2001), chef of the Roma restaurant in Tolmezzo, who codified and revived its traditional recipes. He rescued the cooking of the mountain housewives and brought it to the attention of great gastronomy: Luigi Veronelli called him «the most modern cook Italy has ever had».
tiramisu.p1: The dessert that travelled the world
tiramisu.p2: There is a well-documented and widely supported claim that tiramisù was born right here, in Tolmezzo, a few kilometres from Socchieve. It is said to have been created in the winter of 1951 by Norma Pielli of the Albergo Roma, who transformed the “Dolce Torino”: mascarpone and bitter coffee in place of butter and liqueur. It was her husband, Beppino Del Fabbro, who christened it: tasting it, he said it was «a dessert that lifts you up» — “che tira su”.
tiramisu.p3: Period documents support this origin: a menu of the Albergo Roma dated 9 April 1952, in which the dessert appears as “trancia al mascarpone”, the name under which it was born. In 2017 the Italian Ministry recognised tiramisù among the traditional products of Friuli Venezia Giulia, in its two variants of Tolmezzo and Pieris.
tiramisu.p4: The paternity of the dessert is contested — Treviso claims it too — and the dispute is still open. But one detail ties everything together: the Albergo Roma of the Del Fabbro family is the same restaurant that, from 1969, passed to Gianni Cosetti.
tiramisu.p5: Sources: Il Cibario del Friuli Venezia Giulia, ERSA 2017 (tiramisù among the PAT products); historical documentation of the Albergo Roma in Tolmezzo (menu of 9 April 1952).
assaggiare.p2: During your stay at Il Bearč, you will find cjarsons, frico and the cured meats of Sauris in the trattorias and farmhouse restaurants of the valleys around Socchieve. The best moment to discover them all is the Festa dei Cjarsons in Sutrio, on the first Sunday of June: ten variations of the dish, one for each Carnian village.
assaggiare.p3: Do ask us for advice: we will be happy to point you to the best places nearby to eat the traditional dishes.
